question.text: Under SEBI SAST Regulations 2011, which of the following regarding voluntary open offers is INCORRECT?

answer.text: An acquirer holding between 25% and the maximum non-public shareholding may make a voluntary offer
An acquirer who acquired shares in the preceding 52 weeks without triggering an open offer is eligible for a voluntary offer
Post-completion, the acquirer must not exceed maximum non-public shareholding
The acquirer cannot acquire shares outside the open offer during the offer period

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Regulation 6, an acquirer who acquired shares in the preceding 52 weeks without triggering an open offer is NOT eligible to make a voluntary offer. This is the 52-week disentitlement provision that prevents gaming — acquiring shares quietly below thresholds and then making a voluntary offer. **The Trap:** All other options are correct statements about voluntary offers. The question tests whether candidates understand the 52-week look-back disqualification. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX tests voluntary offer eligibility conditions in detail. The 52-week look-back is one of the most commonly tested provisions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Before initiating a voluntary offer, conduct a thorough audit of all share acquisitions (including by PACs) in the preceding 52 weeks. Bonus issues and stock splits are excluded from this check.
